stories.title: Bronx STEAM Sets Up Shop at Simone’s Hutchinson Metro Center Atrium
stories.excerpt: Simone Development Companies announced that the New York City School Construction Authority has signed a long-term lease to establish the new Bronx STEAM Center in 54,245 square feet across two floors at the Hutchinson Metro Center Atrium, a three-story, 361,857 square-foot mixed-use property located at 1776 Eastchester Rd. in the Bronx. The new lease marks a major public-private investment in career-connected education for Bronx students, with Bronx STEAM and Montefiore Medical Center partnering to launch offerings in high‑demand healthcare and technology pathways, including Certified Nursing Assistant, Cybersecurity and Medical Assisting. “Bringing the Bronx STEAM Center to the Hutchinson Metro Center is incredibly meaningful not only for participating students and for our campus, but for the borough as a whole,” said Joe Simone, president of Simone Development Companies. “This transformative project is more than a new educational facility. It reflects the power of public-private collaboration to create long-term opportunities in the Bronx.” The post Bronx STEAM Sets Up Shop at Simone’s Hutchinson Metro Center Atrium appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: NYC School Construction Authority leases 54,245 SF at Simone's Hutchinson Metro Center in Bronx.
Bronx STEAM partners with Montefiore Medical Center for healthcare and tech career pathways.
Public-private investment signals institutional confidence in Bronx education and employment infrastructure.
stories.summary: The NYC School Construction Authority signed a long-term lease for 54,245 square feet at Simone Development's Hutchinson Metro Center in the Bronx to establish the Bronx STEAM Center, a career-connected education facility. The center will offer nursing, cybersecurity, and medical assistant training in partnership with Montefiore Medical Center. This marks a major public-private commitment to workforce development in the borough.
stories.impact: Institutional education and healthcare investment in the Bronx signals growing employer presence and job creation pipelines in the borough. This attracts working-age population inflow and strengthens rental demand fundamentals for multifamily development in the Bronx—particularly near employment hubs and transit corridors like Hutchinson.

stories.title: Kidder Closes Hollywood Multifamily Sale Despite Softening Rental Trends
stories.excerpt: Kidder Mathews arranged the $7.6-million sale of The Carlton Apartments, a 36-unit property located at 5535 Carlton Way in Hollywood. EVP Darin Beebower and associate Dakoda Iversen represented Los Angeles-based James Investment Partners in the sale to a local real estate investor. The property generated more than 25 offers from prospective buyers despite softening rental trends in parts of the Hollywood market, which have led many investors to more closely scrutinize underwriting assumptions and future rent growth projections. “This transaction is a good example of the importance of persistence,” said Beebower. “We remained committed to the process, stayed engaged with the market and were ultimately able to identify the right buyer to help our client achieve a successful sale.” The post Kidder Closes Hollywood Multifamily Sale Despite Softening Rental Trends appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: James Investment Partners sold 36-unit Carlton Apartments in Hollywood for $7.6M despite softening rents.
Property received 25+ offers despite deteriorating rental trends and tightened underwriting in market.
Hollywood rental market facing absorption and competitive pressure on pricing assumptions.
stories.summary: Kidder Mathews closed the $7.6 million sale of The Carlton Apartments, a 36-unit property in Hollywood, for James Investment Partners despite acknowledged softening rental trends in the submarket. The property attracted over 25 offers, but the market context reflects investor caution around rent growth and higher scrutiny of underwriting models. The deal underscores ongoing buyer interest but rising skepticism on near-term rent upside.
stories.impact: Softening Hollywood rents and tightened cap rate expectations signal market saturation and slower absorption. This raises cost-of-capital for new development in the submarket and pressures density and lease-up assumptions, particularly for mid-market Class B/C assets; developers should expect compressed returns and longer hold periods.

stories.title: NYC DOT Modernizes Willets Point Offices and Yard with $121M Redevelopment
stories.excerpt: Construction is proceeding on the $121-million Harper Street DOT administration building and yard redevelopment project at 32-11 Harper St. in the Willets Point section of Queens. The project team includes the New York City Department of Design and Construction, New York City Department of Transportation (DOT) and the design-build team of lead architect Urbahn Architects, design excellence architect Selldorf Architects and builder Scalamandre-Tully JV. To replace obsolete and deteriorated facilities built in 1934 that no longer met modern operational, safety, or resiliency standards, NYC DOT is redeveloping its existing 160,000-square-foot Queens Roadway Repair and Maintenance & Fleet Services maintenance yard . The project includes the design and construction of three new buildings: a 72,820-square-foot, five-story administration and personnel building and warehouse; an 1,849-square-foot, two-story washbay structure; and a 100-square-foot precast concrete flood- and storm-resistant security booth. “The DOT’s 160,000-square-foot yard operates 24/7 and houses over 300 Roadway Repair & Maintenance and Fleet Services field employees, including warehousing and administration personnel,” said Urbahn associate principal Ijeoma D. Iheanacho, NOMA, AIA, LEED AP, “The redesigned facility will address the current deficiencies and limitations, including creating more efficient workflows and traffic pathways, increasing on-site safety for pedestrians and vehicles, increasing protection from flooding and implementing sustainable strategies to meet LEED Gold certification.” The post NYC DOT Modernizes Willets Point Offices and Yard with $121M Redevelopment appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: NYC DOT $121M redevelopment of Willets Point facility in Queens includes three new buildings.
160,000-SF yard upgraded to 72,820-SF admin building, washbay, security booth; LEED Gold certified.
Public facility modernization reflects Queens infrastructure investment and operational resilience priorities.
stories.summary: NYC Department of Transportation is redeveloping its 160,000-square-foot Willets Point maintenance yard in Queens with a $121 million construction project that includes a new 72,820-square-foot five-story admin building, washbay, and flood-resistant structures. The project, designed by Urbahn and Selldorf Architects, targets LEED Gold certification and addresses operational safety, efficiency, and climate resilience for 300+ employees operating 24/7.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily development. However, large public capital infrastructure spending in Queens (Willets Point/Jamaica/Forest Hills corridor) signals sustained city investment in the borough's operational base, which can support long-term commercial and residential mixed-use development and transit-oriented growth in adjacent submarkets.

stories.title: Locally Based Logistics Firm Scales Up with Menifee Warehouse Acquisition
stories.excerpt: Colliers finalized the $40.5-million sale of Gateway at Menifee, a 229,934-square-foot industrial facility located at 33520 Zeiders Rd. in Menifee. The property is Building 3 within the five-building industrial complex, formerly known as Scott Road Commerce Center. EVP Rick Nunez, SIOR, associate Mateo Mobilia and senior client services specialist Michael Romero represented the buyer, Temecula-based logistics and distribution company Southwest Traders), in the acquisition of the asset from the seller, Scott Road Property, LLC. The deal moved at an accelerated pace, with escrow opening in May 2026, and both parties executed a quick close. “The Menifee facility represents a critical next step in our growth strategy,” said Terry Walsh, president of Southwest Traders. “It will allow us to scale our operations and expand our regional logistics footprint. In addition to the acquisition, we plan to invest approximately $25 million to retrofit a portion of the building into a state-of-the-art refrigerated distribution center.” The post Locally Based Logistics Firm Scales Up with Menifee Warehouse Acquisition appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Southwest Traders acquired Gateway at Menifee (229,934 SF) for $40.5M, plans $25M retrofit investment.
Temecula-based logistics firm scaling regional operations with refrigerated distribution center conversion.
Industrial capital deployment signals strong last-mile logistics demand in Inland Empire.
stories.summary: Colliers facilitated the $40.5 million sale of Gateway at Menifee, a 229,934-square-foot industrial building, to Southwest Traders, a Temecula-based logistics firm. The buyer plans to invest approximately $25 million to retrofit a portion of the facility into a state-of-the-art refrigerated distribution center as part of a broader growth and regional scaling strategy.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily. However, strong logistics capital deployment and e-commerce distribution expansion in the Inland Empire (Menifee/Riverside County) underscore robust job creation in the region, supporting population inflow and rental demand growth for multifamily near logistics hubs and corridors.

stories.title: Basis Industrial Closes on Construction Loan for Wayne, NJ Self-Storage
stories.excerpt: Basis Industrial, a privately held and vertically integrated real estate owner and operator based in Delray Beach, FL closed on a $24-million construction loan on June 18, 2026, for a ground-up self-storage development located at 555 Hamburg Tpke. in Wayne, N.J. The project will encompass approximately 85,330 square feet and 586 climate-controlled storage units upon completion. The loan was provided by NexBank and NexPoint. Construction is expected to begin in August 2026, with delivery anticipated in February 2028. The project will deliver a storage solution to one of northern New Jersey’s most supply-constrained markets. “After more than three years of working through the approval process, we are pleased to reach this milestone and begin construction,” said Anthony Scavo, president and managing partner of Basis Industrial. “Wayne is a market with strong demographics and limited opportunities for new self-storage development, making it an attractive addition to our growing pipeline.” The post Basis Industrial Closes on Construction Loan for Wayne, NJ Self-Storage appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Basis Industrial closed $24M construction loan for 85,330-SF self-storage in Wayne, NJ; delivery Feb 2028.
Project delivers 586 climate-controlled units to supply-constrained northern New Jersey market.
3+ year entitlement process cleared; lender NexBank and NexPoint; construction begins August 2026.
stories.summary: Basis Industrial secured a $24 million construction loan from NexBank and NexPoint for an 85,330-square-foot ground-up self-storage development in Wayne, New Jersey, encompassing 586 climate-controlled units. After more than three years navigating approvals, construction is set to begin in August 2026, with delivery expected in February 2028. The project targets a supply-constrained market with strong demographics.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily. However, the 3+ year entitlement cycle and financing success in northern New Jersey indicate sustained capital appetite for real estate in the region despite regulatory burden. This signals stable credit conditions and investor confidence in the NJ market, supporting ancillary multifamily financing and development feasibility nearby.

stories.title: Fintech Firm Digital Asset Returns to 4 WTC With 19K-SF Lease
stories.excerpt: It’s a full-circle moment for fintech firm Digital Asset. The company has signed a 19,000-square-foot lease at 4 World Trade Center, Commercial Observer has learned. Digital Asset will return to the same building it occupied from 2018 until it moved to 107 Greenwich Street in March 2025. The new lease length is for 10 years, with an asking rent of $115 per square foot, a source close to the deal told CO. Savills‘ Slava Vaynberg and Kirill Azovtsev represented the tenant in the deal, while landlord Silverstein Properties was represented in-house by Harlan Strader and Keith Cody. Savills and Silverstein declined to comment. Founded in 2014, Digital Asset creates software for digital asset management and financial markets. In 2025, CO reported that the company signed a 10-year lease for 12,000 square feet on the entire 17th floor of Trinity Church Wall Street’s 107 Greenwich Street after subleasing temporary space at 4 WTC. It’s unclear why Digital Asset has decided to return to 4 WTC. The company did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Built in 2013, 4 WTC, also known as 150 Greenwich Street, is a 72-story, 2.3 million-square-foot office tower in Lower Manhattan. Other tenants at the office skyscraper include global investment firm KKR, investment research firm Morningstar, and music streaming service Spotify. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Digital Asset signed 19,000-SF lease at 4 WTC, Manhattan; 10-year term at $115/SF asking rent.
Fintech firm returns to 4 WTC after brief 15-month stint at 107 Greenwich Street in 2025.
Manhattan office market absorbing tech tenant relocations; 10-year commitments signal confidence recovery.
stories.summary: Fintech firm Digital Asset has leased 19,000 square feet at 4 World Trade Center in Manhattan for 10 years at an asking rent of $115 per square foot. The company returns to the building it occupied from 2018 until relocating to 107 Greenwich Street in March 2025, marking a reversal in tenant flight patterns. Silverstein Properties was represented in-house; Savills represented the tenant.
stories.impact: Digital Asset's return to 4 WTC signals cautious stabilization in Lower Manhattan office markets and potential early signs of flight reversal among tech tenants. While modest, this supports office valuations and leasing pace near Battery Park, reducing pressure on conversion/adaptive reuse scenarios and suggesting multifamily supply pipeline may remain constrained in FiDi-adjacent neighborhoods dependent on office-to-residential conversions.

stories.title: Gauging the Obama Center’s real estate ripple effects across the South Side
stories.excerpt: The opening of the Obama Presidential Center on the Juneteenth holiday marks the culmination of a project that had construction costs balloon from an initial $300 million estimate to a staggering $850 million, following a half-decade legal battle with park preservation advocates, and pandemic-related construction delays. Envisioned as a 19-acre economic engine for Chicago’s South Side, the center features a towering 225-foot museum building wrapped in concrete letters from the former two-term president’s historic Selma speech. While the center’s backers have promised it would be a long-term boon for tourism and the area’s economic infrastructure, it has simultaneously accelerated real […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Obama Presidential Center opened (cost $850M vs. $300M initial estimate); drove East Woodlawn home prices from $220K to $440K median (2019–2025).
Real estate speculation and outside developer investment accelerated; new construction displaced older brick multifamily stock.
Chicago South Side experiencing gentrification wave; tenant rights battles and displacement pressures intensifying.
stories.summary: The Obama Presidential Center's opening on Chicago's South Side, after cost overruns from $300 million to $850 million, has catalyzed significant real estate appreciation and speculation. East Woodlawn single-family and multifamily prices doubled to a median of $440,000 between 2019 and 2025—far exceeding Illinois state growth rates. Outside developers have acquired sites and constructed luxury townhomes, replacing older multifamily properties and altering the neighborhood's character while sparking fierce tenant rights and displacement battles.
stories.impact: Obama Center's economic gravitational pull is rapidly commodifying the South Side (60637 ZIP), creating a narrow window for acquisition and development in adjacent, less-appreciated submarkets before speculative premiums spread. However, intensified tenant protections and displacement litigation raise entitlement, financing, and operational costs; developers must factor regulatory friction and community opposition into proformas and timelines in gentrifying Chicago South Side corridors.

stories.title: Terra, BH to pay $100M for billionaire Jeff Greene’s West Palm land
stories.excerpt: David Martin’s Terra and BH Group are teaming up to build waterfront high-rise condos on a West Palm Beach site that they’re set to buy from billionaire Jeff Greene.  Greene is selling the property for about $100 million, a source told The Real Deal.  The developer duo have plans for a pair of 31-story condo towers at the 1.6-acre site at 2085 North Flagler Drive, the South Florida Business Journal reported. The land is near Northwood Village and across from the waterfront Currie Park, which is undergoing a $35 million renovation. Records show Greene’s LLC paid $1.5 million for the […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Terra and BH Group acquiring West Palm Beach waterfront site (1.6 acres) from Jeff Greene for $100M; purchased for $1.5M in 2014.
Two 31-story condo towers planned (281 units, 438,300 SF); average unit 1,760 SF; nearby Currie Park undergoing $35M renovation.
Related Group and BH Group also developing adjacent $34M Rosewood Residences site; waterfront condo cluster forming.
stories.summary: Terra and BH Group are acquiring a 1.6-acre waterfront parcel at 2085 North Flagler Drive in West Palm Beach from billionaire Jeff Greene for approximately $100 million, a 6,567 percent appreciation from Greene's $1.5 million 2014 purchase. The developers plan two 31-story condo towers with 281 units, averaging 1,760 square feet, plus 4,400 square feet of retail. The site benefits from proximity to Currie Park's $35 million renovation and adjacent Related Group/BH Rosewood development.
stories.impact: Strong waterfront condo absorption and capital deployment in West Palm Beach—combined with institutional anchor investments (Currie Park $35M public renovation)—signal tight supply and pricing power in the submarket. For multifamily renters, this condo activity and rising land costs may compress affordable rental supply; developers should prioritize workforce-housing density and mixed-income strategies to remain competitive and navigate demand bifurcation in West Palm Beach's increasingly luxury-oriented waterfront corridor.

stories.title: L.A. Closer to Scaling Back ‘Mansion Tax’
stories.excerpt: Los Angeles lawmakers are moving toward asking voters to scale back perhaps the most controversial part of the city’s real estate regulations. The Los Angeles City Council voted 9 to 5 Wednesday to direct the city attorney to draft a ballot measure that would amend Measure ULA, the city’s so-called “mansion tax,” by exempting newly built multifamily properties sold within 10 years of construction, the Los Angeles Times reported. The draft will need to return to the council for final approval before landing on the November ballot. Measure ULA, which took effect in April 2023, imposes a 4 percent tax on nearly all property sales above $5.3 million, and a 5.5 percent tax on sales of $10.6 million or more. But, contrary to its nickname, the tax applies to all commercial assets, and developers and analysts have argued that it has been devastating to multifamily development. Under the ballot measure proposal, the transfer tax would be amended so it would not apply to new apartment buildings sold within 10 years of construction. The tax was initially designed to fund affordable housing construction and homelessness prevention, but the new ballot measure will also allow ULA revenue to be spent on temporary housing. Measure ULA has raised $1.19 billion for affordable housing production, tenant rental assistance, education and eviction defense. That’s less than half of the $2.7 billion that proponents estimated would be generated by early 2026. ULA opponents say the extra tax has significantly dissuaded multifamily investment and is worsening the affordability crisis by suppressing new supply. Data from think tank Rand Corporation found that Measure ULA has cut high-value real estate deals by 31 percent, and has had an even worse effect on commercial properties: Multifamily and commercial transactions fell more than 46 percent since the tax’s 2023 inception. Rand also reported that ULA reduced the production of large multifamily developments by 30 percent. Cityview CEO Sean B
bullets: LA City Council voted 9–5 to draft ballot measure exempting newly built multifamily from Measure ULA 'mansion tax' (4–5.5%) if sold within 10 years.
Measure ULA (April 2023) applies 4% tax on $5.3M+ sales, 5.5% on $10.6M+ sales across all commercial assets.
Proposed exemption targets developer financing and syndication constraints on new apartment supply.
stories.summary: The Los Angeles City Council voted 9–5 to direct the city attorney to draft a ballot measure that would exempt newly constructed multifamily properties sold within 10 years of completion from Measure ULA, the city's controversial transfer tax (4–5.5% on sales above $5.3 million). The exemption aims to address developer and analyst concerns that the tax has constrained multifamily development. The draft ballot measure is expected to return to council for final approval before the November election.
stories.impact: Proposed Measure ULA exemption signals political recognition that the tax has materially impaired multifamily development economics and exit planning in LA. If passed, the exemption lowers effective cost of capital for new apartment development and syndication, improving underwriting returns and development starts. However, passage is not assured; developers should model both scenario (exemption vs. full tax) and monitor ballot measure dynamics closely, as the tax remains a significant headwind to LA multifamily feasibility and timing.

stories.title: Miami-Area Warehouse Trades for $56M
stories.excerpt: Terreno Realty on Thursday announced it has paid $56.3 million for an industrial property in Hialeah Gardens, Fla. The 98,000-square-foot distribution building sits on 16.8 acres at 10910 Northwest 144th Street, adjacent to the intersection of Florida’s Turnpike and Okeechobee Road, Terreno Realty said in a statement. The property includes 36-foot ceilings, nine dock-high doors, six grade-level loading positions and parking for 596 cars. The building is fully leased to an e-commerce firm, but the buyer did not immediately disclose the name of the tenant. The estimated capitalization rate is 5 percent. The seller was Clarion Partners of New York, according to property records. It’s the first sale of the building, which was constructed in 2022. As of the end of 2025, Terreno Realty owned 41 buildings in the Miami area totaling 4.7 million square feet, according to its most recent annual report. Those warehouses had an occupancy rate of 92.2 percent. Terreno Realty is based in Bellevue, Wash. The company operates industrial real estate in six U.S. markets: New York City/Northern New Jersey, Los Angeles, Miami, the San Francisco Bay Area, Seattle and Greater Washington, D.C. It owns more than 300 properties in those markets. Jeff Ostrowski can be reached at jostrowski@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Terreno Realty acquired 98,000-SF industrial building in Hialeah Gardens, FL for $56.3M; 5% cap rate.
Property on 16.8 acres at FL Turnpike and Okeechobee Road intersection; 36-foot ceilings, 9 dock doors; fully leased to e-commerce tenant.
Terreno now owns 41 buildings (4.7M SF) in Miami area with 92.2% occupancy as of end-2025.
stories.summary: Terreno Realty purchased a 98,000-square-foot distribution facility in Hialeah Gardens, Florida, for $56.3 million at an estimated 5 percent cap rate. The property sits on 16.8 acres near the intersection of Florida's Turnpike and Okeechobee Road and features 36-foot ceilings, nine dock-high doors, and is fully leased to an e-commerce tenant. This acquisition represents Terreno's continued portfolio expansion in Miami, where it now owns 41 buildings totaling 4.7 million square feet with 92.2% occupancy.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily. However, robust industrial capital deployment and e-commerce logistics expansion in Miami (Hialeah Gardens, Doral corridor) reinforce strong job creation and worker population growth in South Florida. This supports downstream multifamily rental demand, particularly near logistics hubs and commute corridors serving warehouse and distribution centers.

stories.title: Costco, multifamily on tap as mixed-use projects power Pompano Beach
stories.excerpt: Two major development proposals — a 163,000-square-foot Costco Wholesale and a 132-unit multifamily building — are set to go before Pompano Beach planners next week, adding to the long list of projects underway in the city. The proposed Costco store would sit on a 18.7-acre vacant parcel The Pompwithin the northwest corner of The Pomp, a 223-acre planned commercial development at Racetrack Road and Powerline Road, according to city documents. David Cordish’s The Cordish Companies and Thomas Reeg’s Caesars Entertainment are partnering on The Pomp, a mixed-use hospitality development that will include 1.3 million square feet of retail and entertainment […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Cordish Companies and Caesars Entertainment advancing Costco (163,000 SF) and 132-unit multifamily at The Pomp, Pompano Beach mixed-use.
The Pomp master plan: 1.3M SF retail/entertainment, 1.4M SF office, 4,000 luxury residential units on 223 acres.
Vera Fund also building 132-unit multifamily on Federal Highway (land acquired 2024 for $12.75M); mixed-use retail/office integration.
stories.summary: The Cordish Companies and Caesars Entertainment are seeking approvals for a 163,000-square-foot Costco and a separate 132-unit multifamily building at The Pomp, a 223-acre mixed-use development in Pompano Beach anchored by 1.3 million square feet of retail/entertainment, 1.4 million square feet of office, and 4,000 luxury residential units. Separately, developer Nick Polyushkin's Vera Fund is planning a 132-unit multifamily at 1600 South Federal Highway, acquired in 2024 for $12.75 million, with 3,700 square feet of retail space.
stories.impact: Dual multifamily projects in Pompano Beach (264 units combined) underscore strong rental demand and developer confidence in South Florida's growth trajectory. Large mixed-use anchors (Costco, Caesars) and institutional capital (Cordish, Caesars) signal sustained demographic inflow and leasing velocity; competitive multifamily supply is accelerating. Developers should expect tightening margins and higher lease-up risk if market absorption slows; focus on differentiation, location, and early delivery to capture rental momentum in the Pompano corridor.

stories.title: Vilson Lumaj Plans Two 99-Unit Buildings in the Bronx After $14M Deal
stories.excerpt: Housing developer Vilson Lumaj has purchased a vacant property in the Bronx to build two 99-unit residential buildings, Commercial Observer has learned. The property at 761-769 East Tremont Avenue was sold for $10 million by real estate developer Yoram Eliyahu. The buyer also acquired 60,000 square feet of air rights above the property for $3.5 million from Michael and Rudolf Abramov, according to broker Rosewood Realty Group. The total sale price was $13.5 million. The development plans for two 99-unit buildings were meant to cut down on labor costs while still getting more units on the market, according to Rosewood’s Jonah Corney. “The development is going to be two 99-unit buildings which will be a work-around for the prevailing wage required under 485-x,” Corney told CO. “I guess it’s a sign of poor policy.” Corney represented the buyer alongside Rosewood’s Mike Kerwin and Mark Steinmetz. The proliferation of 99-unit developments throughout New York City has resulted in criticism of the state government for allegedly creating a piece of flawed policy after the expiration of the 421-a property tax abatement, as under its successor, the 485-x tax program, construction costs can increase 20 to 30 percent, depending on who you ask. Critics, such as the Real Estate Board of New York, have said that the tax program prevents the city from reaching its goals of meeting the needs of the national housing shortage. But it appears developers are simply building 99 units across multiple buildings, such as with Ami Weinstock working on six 99-unit buildings in Jamaica, Queens, in 2025, along with a scattering of other projects. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Vilson Lumaj purchased vacant Bronx site (761 East Tremont) for $10M; acquired 60,000 SF air rights for $3.5M; total $13.5M.
Developer planning two 99-unit buildings to circumvent prevailing wage requirements under NY 485-x housing law.
Proliferation of 99-unit developments signals policy-driven workaround; developer criticism of state labor mandates.
stories.summary: Housing developer Vilson Lumaj acquired a vacant property at 761–769 East Tremont Avenue in the Bronx for $10 million, plus 60,000 square feet of air rights for $3.5 million (total $13.5 million), to develop two 99-unit residential buildings. The two-building structure is explicitly designed to avoid prevailing wage requirements under New York's 485-x law, which mandates higher labor costs for larger developments. Brokers cited this as a sign of poor state policy creating perverse development incentives.
stories.impact: Proliferation of 99-unit projects in NYC (to dodge 485-x prevailing wage mandates) distorts density and site economics, fragmenting supply and reducing per-site yields. For developers, this signals that regulatory labor cost burdens are material enough to reshape project architecture and unit counts—eroding competitive advantage and discouraging larger, more efficient developments. Monitor state labor policy closely; changes to prevailing wage thresholds or carve-outs could unlock larger, more viable projects in the Bronx and outer boroughs.

stories.title: Related, Battery Park City Authority to Expand Affordable Housing at Tribeca Park
stories.excerpt: The Knicks parade wasn’t the only noise happening in Manhattan’s Battery Park on Thursday. Related Companies and the Battery Park City Authority (BPCA) have teamed up to preserve and expand the number of affordable units at Tribeca Park, Related’s 27-story Lower Manhattan residential building, the partners announced Thursday. Related built the 369-unit rental tower at 400 Chambers Street near Battery Park in 1999. The new agreement with BPCA amends the building’s ground lease to keep in place the 81 units already designated as affordable housing for individuals earning 50 percent of the area medium income. The agreement also will add 20 more units for people earning up to 130 percent of the area medium income. “We’re proud to work with Related to preserve and increase the number of affordable units in Tribeca Park,” Raju Mann, president and CEO of BPCA, said in a statement. “BPCA is committed to using any and every opportunity we have to add affordable homes to this neighborhood.” As part of the agreement, 101 units, or 25 percent of the building’s total residences, will be income-restricted through the remainder of the building’s lease term in 2069. Previously, the affordability at 400 Chambers Street was going to expire at the end of 2029. The agreement also increases the building’s ground rent payments to BPCA. “Battery Park City stands as one of New York City’s most successful mixed-income communities, and Related has been honored to be part of its evolution for more than four decades,” Bruce A. Beal Jr., president of Related Companies, said in a statement. “Maintaining and expanding affordable housing is critical to the city’s long-term economic strength and quality of life.” Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Related Companies and Battery Park City Authority (BPCA) amended Tribeca Park ground lease to preserve 81 affordable units (50% AMI) and add 20 more (130% AMI).
27-story rental tower (369 units total) at 400 Chambers Street; 1999 construction; long-term affordability lock-in.
BPCA and Related partnering to expand affordable housing in Lower Manhattan as regulatory tool.
stories.summary: Related Companies and the Battery Park City Authority announced an amendment to the ground lease for Tribeca Park, the 27-story, 369-unit rental tower at 400 Chambers Street, to preserve 81 units designated as affordable for individuals earning 50 percent of area median income and add 20 additional units for those earning up to 130 percent AMI. The agreement reflects a public-private partnership to expand affordable housing in Lower Manhattan and secure long-term affordability commitments.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on market-rate multifamily development. However, BPCA-negotiated affordability deals on established properties signal regulatory appetite for extracting affordable units from existing portfolios. Developers should expect similar affordability demands in Lower Manhattan ground leases and refinancing negotiations; factor long-term rent-controlled unit drag into proformas and exit planning for Battery Park City and waterfront Manhattan assets.

stories.title: Universal Music plans first US branded condo project at contentious Hill Country site
stories.excerpt: Branded condo development is taking off in Texas, and Universal Music Group is tossing its hat in the ring.  The “big three” record label is partnering with Austin developer White Rocks Entertainment to build UMusic Hotel & Private Residences Austin at a Hill Country site near Bee Cave, the Austin Business Journal reported.  The project, at 10549 SH 71, will feature 600 condo units, 150 hotel rooms and a 10,000-seat outdoor venue. Proposed amenities include professional recording studios, a nightclub, comedy club, indoor performance venue and two restaurants. It would be the first stateside hospitality project from Universal, which has […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Universal Music Group partnering with White Rocks Entertainment for UMusic Hotel & Private Residences Austin near Bee Cave (SH 71).
600 condo units, 150 hotel rooms, 10,000-seat outdoor venue; construction 2027–2030; units start at $2.3M; 100% water reuse target.
White Rocks' prior Violet Crown project (luxury apartments, amphitheater) killed over environmental opposition; new project redesigned for water/environmental compliance.
stories.summary: Universal Music Group is partnering with Austin developer White Rocks Entertainment to build UMusic Hotel & Private Residences Austin at a Hill Country site near Bee Cave. The project features 600 condo units, 150 hotel rooms, a 10,000-seat outdoor venue, and recording studios, with unit pricing starting at $2.3 million. Construction is scheduled to begin in early 2027 and conclude in 2030, contingent on approvals. The project incorporates a 100 percent water reuse standard and 70+ percent open space, addressing environmental concerns that derailed the prior Violet Crown project.
stories.impact: Universal Music's branded condo venture signals institutional entertainment capital flowing into Austin hill country and experiential mixed-use development. High average unit pricing ($2.3M+) targets luxury cache and international capital, not working-age renters. For multifamily developers, this underscores Austin market bifurcation: branded/experiential luxury projects are capturing capital and attention, while price-point competition and feasibility challenges intensify in workforce-housing segments; focus on infill, transit access, and cost efficiency to compete for capital in Austin.

stories.title: Real estate getting drama treatment at Hulu
stories.excerpt: Real estate’s dramatics know no bounds, which will be on full display with a forthcoming Hulu series. George Stephanopoulos and Ali Wentworth are executive producing “The Pinnacle” at the streaming series, Deadline reported. The drama is being written by Damon Cardasis, who has been nominated for multiple awards.  The series — which licenses content from The Real Deal in its pilot — is set in New York City and follows the journey of a man who becomes exposed to the upper class while serving as a sobriety companion to the son of a wealthy family that lives on Billionaires’ Row. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Hulu series 'The Pinnacle' in development; George Stephanopoulos and Ali Wentworth executive producing; set on Manhattan Billionaires' Row.
Show follows sobriety companion exposed to wealthy family real estate world; licenses content from The Real Deal.
Prior Hulu real estate drama (Edgar Ramirez, Jacob Scipio as rival Manhattan developers) cancelled; scripted real estate content still emerging.
stories.summary: George Stephanopoulos and Ali Wentworth are executive producing a new Hulu drama series, "The Pinnacle," set in New York City and centered on real estate in the upper-class market, particularly Billionaires' Row. The show follows a man who becomes exposed to wealth while serving as a sobriety companion to the son of a prominent family and licenses content from The Real Deal. Another real estate drama featuring rival Manhattan developers (Edgar Ramirez and Jacob Scipio) is no longer moving forward.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on market fundamentals. However, continued investment in scripted real estate entertainment (despite prior cancellations) signals sustained pop-culture interest in luxury Manhattan real estate, which can amplify brand awareness and aspirational demand among high-net-worth audiences and international buyers—supporting pricing and absorption for luxury condo and penthouses on Billionaires' Row and similar ultra-prime markets.

stories.title: Trademark lassos Whole Foods for Southlake mixed-use development
stories.excerpt: Trademark Property Company officially secured Whole Foods Market to anchor its Southlake mixed-use project.  Terry Montesi’s Fort Worth-based development firm announced that the Austin-based organic grocer is coming to Shivers Farm, the 40-acre mixed-use development it’s advancing on White Chapel Boulevard, according to a release from Trademark. The news comes two days after Southlake City Council approved the project’s master development agreement.  Locals speculated that Whole Foods would anchor the project after a city council member referred to the future grocery store as Whole Foods during an October meeting, the Fort Worth Star-Telegram reported. In the release, the firm said […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Trademark Property Company anchored Shivers Farm mixed-use (40 acres, Southlake) with Whole Foods; construction begins summer 2026, completion end-2027.
Shivers Farm: 111,000 SF retail, 38,000 SF office, 37 single-family lots, 3-acre pad for hotel/retail, 1-acre park.
Dallas retail pipeline 7M SF; 5M SF pre-leased; Trademark also developing Westbend and Waterside (another Whole Foods-anchored project) in Fort Worth.
stories.summary: Trademark Property Company officially secured Whole Foods Market as the anchor tenant for Shivers Farm, a 40-acre mixed-use development in Southlake on White Chapel Boulevard. The project will feature 111,000 square feet of retail, 38,000 square feet of office, 37 single-family lots, a three-acre pad approved for a hotel or retail use, and a one-acre park. Construction is scheduled to begin summer 2026 and wrap by end-2027. This is the first major grocer approved north of State Highway 114 in Southlake.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily. However, Whole Foods anchoring major mixed-use development in Southlake (affluent North Dallas suburb) signals sustained suburban retail appetite and demographic strength in the DFW metro. Trademark's Whole Foods strategy (Shivers Farm and Waterside in Fort Worth) mirrors broader North Texas retail resilience and mixed-use density trends; strong suburban economics support adjacency multifamily development and support demand for rental housing near shopping and office anchors.
